svg.title: AuthController.login() function-to-code execution map
svg.desc: Strict execution-order diagram for Product Portal login, including startup configuration, runtime method calls, database touchpoints, alternate paths, and complete failure branches from cause through HTTP response.
svg.text: Top-to-bottom success order. Red nodes terminate, amber nodes are alternates, green nodes are DB touchpoints, and the lower matrix expands every failure.
Not invoked per login request; the built filter chain is used at runtime.
Validates path groups before ProviderManager + BCryptPasswordEncoder are used.
Then the prebuilt SecurityFilterChain evaluates jwtBypass and authorization matchers
Not executed for JWT-bypass login endpoint.
permitAll matcher authorizes anonymous access; request proceeds to Spring MVC
UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter does not process this endpoint; form login is disabled
Detailed cause → response flow: F1 matrix row below
9. ProviderManager selects and invokes custom provider
Detailed audit → handler flow: F2 matrix row below
No DB trigger attached to this table
Detailed throttle + audit flow: F3 matrix row below
Detailed throttle + audit flow: F4 matrix row below
No DB trigger attached to either login table
Provider catches CurrentUserAccessDeniedException and records security failure
Requires statusCode ACTIVE and status.active true
Detailed security-audit flow: F5 matrix row below
Detailed security-audit flow: F6 matrix row below
Login reads these tables only. RBAC audit triggers do not fire.
No DB trigger attached to these login tables
No DB trigger attached to this table
Revoke older sessions, then continue; this is not a login failure
No DB trigger attached to this table
Normal login does not fire PostgreSQL database triggers.
Existing triggers are on RBAC/membership mutation tables only:
AOP logs and rethrows; transactional outcome depends on failure point
Detailed transaction effects: F7 matrix row below

g.title: Detailed failure branch execution matrix
g.text: Failure Branches — Cause to Terminal HTTP Response
Each row executes left to right. Red arrows are terminal failure paths; none rejoin the successful login flow.
Spring MVC applies @Valid before controller invocation
AuthController.login() and authentication code never execute
No provider, throttle, audit, session, or JWT work
No login-related repository method is called
No login table is read or written
CorrelationIdFilter still completes its finally cleanup
Validation error map returned to caller
No credential or token data is returned
F2 — Active IP or username block
Existing throttle counters are not incremented
BCrypt matches supplied password against dummy hash
laterOf() selects the furthest lock deadline
User exists and real BCrypt verification runs
RBAC, session creation, and JWT signing are skipped
Same external response as an unknown username
No internal account details are disclosed
Status must be ACTIVE and status.active must be true
Password was valid, but access context is rejected
Provider wraps the cause in DisabledException
No throttle failure counter is incremented
No auth session or token is created
Returns the same generic credential message
External caller cannot infer account status
The resulting active-assignment list is empty
No RBAC DB trigger fires; no session/JWT is created
Any uncaught failure from controller/service execution
Examples: repository, transaction, access-context, session, or JWT failure
Optional login tracer also records the failed method
Validation and known authentication exceptions use F1–F6
The currently failing @Transactional method rolls back
Previously committed service transactions remain committed
Provider success audit commits before session creation
Session transaction outcome depends on failure location
Failure during createSession(): session transaction rolls back
Earlier SUCCESS login audit may already exist
Failure during JWT signing occurs after session commit
Then both SUCCESS audit and auth session may remain
No special generic-failure login audit is authored
buildErrorResponseDto() returns a safe generic message
Credentials, tokens, and internals are not exposed
Repository signatures are authored contracts; Spring Data supplies their executable implementations. Lombok-generated and implicit record methods are intentionally omitted.
A0 and A1 are amber alternate paths, not failures. F1–F7 are terminal and never rejoin the HTTP 200 path.
